Post the profile in a shared public folder so future collaborators can find it without requesting access.
Replace the student name with initials and then send all details through any convenient personal account.
questions.rationale: A classroom teacher must handle identifiable education records through purpose-limited, role-appropriate, secure procedures. Convenience or general employment does not establish legitimate educational interest, and removing a name may not remove identifiability. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When adapting comprehension instruction from evidence, a classroom teacher is offered compensation for promoting one commercial reading resource. What is the responsible response?
questions.promptB: When responding to a diverse reader’s comprehension profile, a classroom teacher receives free materials in exchange for recommending one commercial program. What should the teacher do?
questions.correct: Decline or disclose the benefit as required, follow school ethics and purchasing rules, present objective evidence and alternatives, and recuse from a recommendation when impartiality is compromised.
distractors: Accept the benefit privately because classroom expertise makes disclosure unnecessary.
Recommend the resource to families first and report the compensation only if someone asks later.
Hide the vendor relationship but promise to use the resource only with students who volunteer.
questions.rationale: A classroom teacher protects students and public trust by disclosing or avoiding personal benefits, following school rules, and separating evidence from commercial influence. Recusal is appropriate when a financial interest prevents an impartial recommendation. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When adapting comprehension instruction from evidence, what is an evidence-based way for a classroom teacher to advocate for improved reading access?
questions.promptB: When responding to a diverse reader’s comprehension profile, after hearing one compelling story, what should a classroom teacher contribute before requesting an instructional change?
questions.correct: Combine student and family perspectives with classroom evidence and relevant research, identify the access barrier, propose a feasible instructional change through established channels, and monitor its effects.
distractors: Present the single story as proof that one change will benefit every learner in every classroom.
Wait until all uncertainty disappears before communicating any documented access barrier.
Demand a schoolwide purchase without describing the student need, evidence, implementation, or review plan.
questions.rationale: Classroom advocacy is strongest when the teacher centers affected learners, connects experience with evidence, proposes a role-appropriate instructional response, collaborates through established channels, and checks consequences. One story can prompt inquiry but cannot establish a universal conclusion by itself. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

leadA: When adapting comprehension instruction from evidence,
leadB: When responding to a diverse reader’s comprehension profile,

label: Writing Process, Genres, and Reading–Writing Integration

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, how are reading and writing instruction related?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, why should a literacy plan integrate reading and composing rather than treat them as separate blocks?
questions.correct: Reading provides models and knowledge for writing, while writing deepens analysis of language, text structure, evidence, and meaning.
distractors: Writing practice automatically fixes every decoding need.
Reading and writing share no underlying knowledge.
Composition should begin only after reading is mastered.
questions.rationale: Reading and writing are reciprocal but not identical; effective instruction connects them while directly teaching the distinct transcription, language, comprehension, and composition skills learners need. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, what should writing assessment distinguish?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, a student has strong ideas but writes little because spelling and handwriting are effortful. What should the teacher examine?
questions.correct: Transcription fluency and accuracy separately from planning, language, organization, revision, and purpose.
distractors: Treat total word count as the only writing construct.
Assume limited output means limited knowledge.
Score handwriting appearance as reading comprehension.
questions.rationale: Writing performance reflects interacting transcription and composition demands; separating components reveals whether instruction should address mechanics, language, strategy, access, or several areas. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, what is disciplinary literacy?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, why might reading a laboratory report require different supports from reading a historical argument?
questions.correct: Disciplines use characteristic purposes, evidence, vocabulary, representations, and reasoning that should be taught explicitly.
distractors: Every subject uses identical text conventions.
Content teachers should avoid literacy instruction.
Disciplinary literacy means simplifying all texts.
questions.rationale: Students need general literacy skills and instruction in how experts in a discipline build claims, read sources, interpret representations, and communicate knowledge. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, how should a teacher support digital-text reading?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, students click links rapidly but do not evaluate or integrate information. What instruction is needed?
questions.correct: Teach navigation, source evaluation, lateral verification, synthesis across modes, and monitoring of purpose and attention.
distractors: Assume digital familiarity equals critical literacy.
Ban all hyperlinks without teaching evaluation.
Judge credibility by visual design alone.
questions.rationale: Digital literacy includes comprehension plus navigation, authorship and evidence checks, multimodal integration, privacy awareness, and strategic control of distraction. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, why use coherent text sets?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, a grade team selects several texts around one science concept. What is the strongest rationale?
questions.correct: Connected texts can build vocabulary and knowledge while allowing comparison of genres, claims, evidence, and perspectives.
distractors: Every text must use the same difficulty and viewpoint.
A text set eliminates the need for explicit instruction.
Students should memorize facts without discussing sources.
questions.rationale: Purposefully sequenced text sets support cumulative knowledge and language while creating opportunities for synthesis and critical examination. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, how can sentence-level instruction support literacy?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, students understand ideas orally but struggle with complex written syntax. What should instruction include?
questions.correct: Explicit work with sentence meanings, clauses, connectives, expansion, combining, and deconstruction in reading and writing contexts.
distractors: Label parts of speech without connecting them to meaning.
Reduce every text to short simple sentences permanently.
Teach punctuation as an isolated copying task only.
questions.rationale: Syntax instruction is most useful when students examine how sentence structures express relationships and apply that knowledge while comprehending and composing. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, what makes genre instruction transferable?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, students can fill in one narrative organizer but cannot plan a new story. What should change?
questions.correct: Teach the purposes, flexible structures, language features, and decision making of the genre across varied models and writing tasks.
distractors: Require the same template for every genre.
Focus only on page length.
Avoid mentor texts because students may imitate them.
questions.rationale: Genre knowledge becomes transferable when learners compare examples, discuss author choices, plan for audience and purpose, compose, receive feedback, and revise. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, how should evidence be taught in source-based writing?
questions.promptB: While teaching a recursive writing process, a student inserts quotations but does not connect them to a claim. What feedback is most useful?
questions.correct: Select relevant evidence, introduce and represent it accurately, explain how it supports the claim, and cite the source appropriately.
distractors: Add more quotations without analysis.
Use only the longest available quotation.
Replace the claim with a source summary.
questions.rationale: Evidence functions through relevance and reasoning; source-based writing requires accurate representation, attribution, integration, explanation, and attention to audience and purpose. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

label: Spelling, Sentence Construction, Conventions, and Assessment

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, how do screening and diagnostic assessment differ?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a student scores below a benchmark on a universal reading screener. What does that result justify?
questions.correct: It identifies elevated risk and need for timely follow-up; diagnostic evidence is needed to specify skills and instruction.
distractors: It establishes a disability by itself.
It dictates one commercial intervention.
It proves the student received inadequate instruction.
questions.rationale: Screening prioritizes efficient risk identification, whereas diagnostic assessment samples components and error patterns to guide hypotheses and teaching. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, what is the purpose of progress monitoring?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, why administer a brief comparable reading measure repeatedly during intervention?
questions.correct: To estimate change toward a defined goal and support decisions about continuing, adjusting, or intensifying instruction.
distractors: To rediagnose the student every week.
To replace instruction with testing.
To compare unrelated skills at each administration.
questions.rationale: Progress monitoring uses repeated technically adequate measures of the same broad outcome or defined skill to evaluate responsiveness over time. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, what does reliability mean in assessment?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a reading measure yields highly inconsistent scores under comparable conditions. Which quality is weak?
questions.correct: The consistency or precision of the scores for the intended use.
distractors: The cultural relevance of the book list only.
The instructional intensity of the intervention.
The face attractiveness of the report.
questions.rationale: Reliability concerns score consistency and measurement error; it is necessary but does not by itself establish that an interpretation is valid or fair. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, what does validity mean?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a test produces consistent scores but does not adequately measure the reading construct used for the decision. What is the problem?
questions.correct: Evidence does not support the intended interpretation and use of the scores.
distractors: The test is invalid only because it is reliable.
The student must retake it until the score rises.
Validity is a permanent property independent of purpose.
questions.rationale: Validity concerns evidence for a particular interpretation and use in a defined population and context, not a universal label attached to an instrument. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, why do sensitivity and specificity matter in screening?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a district evaluates a screener?s ability to identify students at risk without referring too many students who are not at risk. What is being balanced?
questions.correct: Correct identification of students with risk and correct exclusion of students without the target risk.
distractors: Interrater reliability and handwriting speed.
Percentile rank and grade equivalent.
Treatment fidelity and social validity.
questions.rationale: Sensitivity and specificity describe classification accuracy; thresholds should reflect prevalence, consequences, resources, equity, and the follow-up pathway. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, how do norm-referenced and criterion-referenced interpretations differ?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a report compares a learner with a representative peer group and also lists mastered spelling patterns. What are the two interpretations?
questions.correct: Norm referencing compares performance with a group; criterion referencing compares performance with defined skills or standards.
distractors: Both comparisons are grade-equivalent scores.
Criterion referencing ranks students only.
Norm referencing proves which lesson to teach next.
questions.rationale: Norms describe relative standing, while criterion information describes performance on specified content; each can contribute different evidence to a decision. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, how should standard error influence score interpretation?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a reading score is reported as if it were perfectly exact. What should the teacher add?
questions.correct: A confidence interval or other explanation of measurement uncertainty and the limits of small score differences.
distractors: A guarantee that the true score equals the obtained score.
A grade-equivalent conversion for every audience.
A new cutoff selected after seeing the result.
questions.rationale: Observed scores contain measurement error; confidence intervals and converging evidence reduce false precision and overinterpretation of minor differences. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, what makes an assessment fair?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a decoding test requires vocabulary and cultural knowledge unrelated to the intended word-reading construct. What should be examined?
questions.correct: Whether construct-irrelevant language, experience, access, administration, or scoring creates systematic barriers to the intended interpretation.
distractors: Whether every learner receives the same score.
Whether the test is short.
Whether all accommodations are prohibited.
questions.rationale: Fairness requires equitable opportunity to demonstrate the target construct and examination of bias, accessibility, consequences, and subgroup evidence. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, how should an accommodation be evaluated?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a student uses an approved support during a reading assessment. What must the team determine?
questions.correct: Whether the support improves access without changing the construct, and how it affects score comparability and interpretation.
distractors: Whether it makes the score higher.
Whether every student uses the same support.
Whether the support can remain undocumented.
questions.rationale: An accommodation should address an access barrier while preserving the intended construct; changes that alter the construct require different interpretation. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

questions.promptA: When using writing evidence to plan explicit instruction, why is administration fidelity important?
questions.promptB: During assessment-guided written-expression instruction, a teacher changes timing and prompts on a standardized measure but reports the standard score. What is wrong?
questions.correct: Departures can invalidate the standard comparison and must be documented with appropriately limited interpretation.
distractors: All informal changes improve validity.
Standard scores remain comparable after any change.
Only the student needs to know about the change.
questions.rationale: Standardized scores depend on specified administration and scoring; accessible changes must follow allowable procedures or be reported as nonstandard. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
